optimist.name: Dawn 'Sunny' Kellerman
optimist.column:
The Padres lost yesterday, but let’s not talk about that. Let’s talk about the sun rising over North Park, the way Machado’s bat path is a sunset, and how this team is built to rise again. Beach ball baseball isn’t about perfection—it’s about momentum, about the kind of grit that turns a loss into a lesson. You see, the Rockies didn’t just beat us; they beat a team that’s already got its eyes on the horizon.  

Look, I know the numbers don’t lie. Ten wins, six losses, two games behind in the West. But here’s the thing: those numbers are just a snapshot. The real story is in the moments you don’t see on the scoreboard. Like when Jake Peavy came out of the bullpen and threw a pitch so sharp it could’ve cut through a surfboard. Or when young guys like Josh Hader and Mike Soroka stepped into the fire and didn’t flinch. This isn’t a team that’s chasing the past—it’s building for the future, one game at a time.  

I’ve watched these players grow since I first walked these streets, handing out sunscreen and surfboards while yelling at the TV about the Padres. They’re not just athletes; they’re part of this community, this family. And families don’t quit. They dig deeper, they rally harder, and they remember why they started. The ring? That’s not a destination—it’s a destination they’re already mapping out.  

Sure

pessimist.name: Lou Catapano
pessimist.column:
The Padres won yesterday. That’s the first thing I’ll say. Colorado Rockies 2, Padres 7. A seven-run outburst, a bullpen that finally looked like it might hold a lead, and a momentary reprieve from the usual madness. But here’s the thing: this isn’t a playoff team. It’s a team that’s 10-6, two games behind the division leader, and still managing to look like it’s one misstep away from another October exit. The Brown Wave, as they call it, is a mirage. It’s always been a mirage.  

You think the win matters? It does. But only in the same way a rainstorm matters in a desert—temporary relief,
